bibliographic.citation: Karl Horstmann, 'Prosalegenden. V. S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio),' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 4 (1881), 109-138.
bibliographic.title: Prosalegenden. V. S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio)

entities.description: Horstmann's Anglia article gives a manuscript contents description and dialect study on pp. 109-115, followed by his edition of the three Middle English prose components on pp. 116-138. He describes Royal 17 C XVII as damaged by damp, faded and written in one hand, and tentatively infers that the Johannes named elsewhere in the codex was the scribe-compiler.
entities.label: Karl Horstmann, S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio) (1881)

rights.basis: The article was published in 1881, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Horstmann's 1881 article, transcription and editorial apparatus

entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century paper miscellany in English and Latin, with tenth-century vellum flyleaves. The Saint Antony compilation occupies fols. 124v-133r; the three components begin at 124v, 126v and 132r, and medical recipes begin at 133v.
entities.label: British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII

notes: The current catalog describes gatherings chiefly of sixteen leaves and the English as Northern (?).
Horstmann reported 162 leaves in the manuscript's then-current state, severe damp damage and fading, and one hand across the codex; the current BL record reports 166 leaves.
The vellum flyleaves are remnants of a tenth-century English Latin lectionary.

rights.basis: The textual works and witness were created centuries before modern copyright. This does not govern modern photographs, transcriptions, editions, interfaces or provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English and Latin textual content

rights.basis: No item-specific public image object or license was available to inspect, and no reproduction has been supplied. Publication, redistribution and commercial-use permission must be established from the terms attached to any future delivery.

entities.description: The Latin translation by Evagrius of Antioch of Athanasius of Alexandria's Greek Life of Antony. Royal 17 C XVII component 20a is a Middle English version of the opening portion, corresponding to chapters 1-13 in the Patrologia Latina printing cited by the BL catalog; the exact Latin exemplar used by the Middle English translator is unknown.
entities.label: Vita Antonii in Evagrius's Latin translation (BHL 609)

entities.description: The anonymous or traditionally attributed Latin legendary account of Antony's hidden body, its discovery by Bishop Theophilus and its first translation to Constantinople. The Middle English rubric associates the account with Theophilus, but this is not treated as verified historical authorship.
entities.label: Finding and first translation of Saint Antony to Constantinople (BHL 612)

entities.description: The Latin legendary account of the relics' movement from Constantinople into the Vienne region, the Montmajour association and the development of Antony's healing cult. It is the source work of the third Middle English component; the exact Latin exemplar remains unidentified.
entities.label: Second translation of Saint Antony into Gaul (BHL 613)

reviewNotes: The intellectual unit is a witness-specific three-part Middle English compilation, not Horstmann's complete article or an undifferentiated life of Antony.
All manuscript-to-Latin relations identify abstract source works; none claims that the anonymous Middle English translator used the later Bollandist printings or any identified surviving Latin codex.
The manuscript rubric's Jerome attribution, Horstmann's compiler inference and competing dialect localizations remain attributed and qualified rather than silently normalized.
Every public supporting PDF used was cached complete and hard-linked to the main checkout; the manuscript remains uncached because no complete public facsimile was verified.

lineage.summary: CME00090 is the checked-in repository copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Karl Horstmann's 1881 edition of a three-part Middle English prose compilation about Antony the Great. The encoding retains the complete medieval text on printed pp. 116-138 but omits Horstmann's manuscript description and dialect study on pp. 109-115. Horstmann transcribed the sole direct witness, British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII, fols. 124v-133r. The current BL component record separates the compilation into a partial English version of the Athanasius-Evagrius life (BHL 609; fols. 124v-126r), the legendary finding and first translation of the relics to Constantinople (BHL 612; fols. 126v-131v), and the second translation into France/Vienne (BHL 613; fols. 132r-133r); medical recipes begin on fol. 133v. A complete 648-page scan of Anglia 4, the complete BHL reference volume, the complete Analecta Bollandiana volume containing BHL 612, and the complete Acta Sanctorum volume containing BHL 613 are cached locally. The BL catalog lists no digitised content for Royal MS 17 C XVII, so no public complete manuscript facsimile is claimed; the record preserves physical-request and reference-enquiry routes and asks for complete whole-object imaging separately from the work's target folios.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator(s)
catalogSummary.date: By the early 15th century; first published 1881
catalogSummary.region: England; witness dialect disputed between Northern (?) and a mixed western Midland analysis
catalogSummary.title: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation

catalogingSubject.label: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the three-part Middle English prose compilation transmitted continuously in British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII, fols. 124v-133r, and edited by Karl Horstmann on printed pp. 116-138. Its parts are a partial life of Antony the Great from the Athanasius-Evagrius tradition (BHL 609), a legendary finding and first translation of his relics to Constantinople (BHL 612), and a second translation into the region of Vienne (BHL 613). CME00090 encodes the complete Middle English edition but excludes Horstmann's introduction and dialect study on pp. 109-115. It is not Horstmann's complete article, the entire manuscript, or one undifferentiated saint's life.

contentParts.label: I. Life of Saint Antony
notes: BHL 609; the BL catalog identifies the translated span as Evagrius's Latin version of Athanasius, chapters 1-13.
The Middle English manuscript rubric's claim that Jerome translated the source from Greek to Latin conflicts with the current catalog and BHL attribution.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English translation of the opening portion of the Athanasian life in Evagrius's Latin tradition: Antony renounces wealth, pursues ascetic and solitary discipline, withstands sexual and demonic temptations, and emerges physically and spiritually unbroken after twenty years in seclusion.

contentParts.label: II. Finding and first translation of Saint Antony's relics
notes: BHL 612. The BL catalog describes the component as the first translation to Constantinople and identifies the Latin account printed in Analecta Bollandiana 2 (1883), pp. 341-354.
Printed p. 122 is shared with the end of part I.
contentParts.summary: A legendary account in which the emperor's demon-possessed daughter Sophia can be cured only through Antony's body. Bishop Theophilus and twelve clerics find the hidden relics in Egypt and bring them through a sequence of miracles to Constantinople, where Sophia is delivered and the body enshrined.

contentParts.label: III. Translation of Saint Antony's relics into the Vienne region
notes: BHL 613. The next codex component, medical recipes, begins on fol. 133v.
Printed p. 135 is shared with the end of part II.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English version of the relic-translation narrative connecting Antony's body with Vienne: Jocelin receives the relics at Constantinople, a descendant entrusts them to monks of Montmajour, a monastery and hospital are founded, and Antony's aid is invoked against the burning illness called Saint Anthony's fire.

assertion.notes: The witness is dated 1400-1440. It supplies a terminus ante quem for the surviving Middle English versions, not a precise translation date.

dateStatements.display: Early fifteenth century (Royal MS 17 C XVII)

extent.description: One DIV1 compilation containing three DIV2 parts, 23 PB elements, 140 NOTE elements, 189 MILESTONE elements, 2,161 HI elements, and 13 P elements. The encoded medieval text covers Horstmann's printed pp. 116-138; no verse L or LG elements occur.

formStatements.description: All three medieval components are prose; the XML contains no L or LG verse elements.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, biblical quotations, and the colophon are embedded in a substantively Middle English prose work and do not make the whole-work language facet multilingual.
Antony is Antony the Great, the Egyptian abbot and hermit, not Anthony of Padua.
The BL catalog's 166-leaf extent supersedes Horstmann's statement that the manuscript then contained 162 leaves; both belong to their dated physical-description layers.
Horstmann's dialect localization and the current catalog's Northern (?) label are preserved as competing witness-language assessments and are not converted into a place of composition.
The many italic HI spans in CME00090 reproduce Horstmann's editorial typography, including supplied or expanded letters and German notes; they are not direct evidence of manuscript emphasis.
The complete Anglia volume, BHL reference volume, Analecta Bollandiana volume, and Acta Sanctorum volume are cached locally. No complete public facsimile of Royal MS 17 C XVII was verified.

next_steps: Consult a modern codicological and paleographical study of Royal MS 17 C XVII.
Compare translation technique and vocabulary across all three parts.
Retain Johannes only as Horstmann's tentative compiler inference until stronger evidence appears.
openQuestions.question: Did one Middle English translator or compiler assemble all three components, and was that person the Johannes Presbyter/Purvare named elsewhere in Royal MS 17 C XVII?

next_steps: Check LALME/eLALME and later linguistic studies for the manuscript's linguistic profile.
Reconcile the current catalog's Northern (?) label with Horstmann's mixed Midland/western proposal.
Keep scribal language separate from composition and translation place.
openQuestions.question: How should the manuscript's dialect be localized in current Middle English dialectology?

next_steps: Ask BL Reference Services about complete whole-object imaging and costs, quoting Royal MS 17 C XVII.
Map the target work separately to fols. 124v-133r in any supplied complete object.
Do not infer publication or repository redistribution permission for supplied images without written terms.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply a complete color digital facsimile of Royal MS 17 C XVII, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every manuscript leaf?

scope.description: Cultural and linguistic origin of the surviving Middle English compilation

assertion.notes: The current BL catalog labels the codex's English 'Northern (?)'.

scope.description: Witness/scribal language, not a proved place of composition

assertion.notes: Horstmann describes mixed Midland inflections and mostly Northern vocalism but tentatively places the dialect in the western group, probably western Middle England.

scope.description: Horstmann's tentative linguistic localization of the witness

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translator or translators

assertion.notes: No source establishes that one person translated all three parts or identifies the exact Latin exemplar.

scope.description: One or more unidentified translators of the three Latin components into Middle English

assertion.notes: Horstmann infers from the same hand and colophon that the scribe named elsewhere as Johannes Presbyter/Purvare may have compiled the unit. The current BL catalog does not make that responsibility certain.

scope.description: Editor of the 1881 immediate source edition
Transcription of Royal MS 17 C XVII, fols. 124v-133r

subjects.label: Saint Anthony's fire and its relief

summaries.value: A three-part Middle English prose compilation about Antony the Great. It moves from an abridged account of his ascetic life and demonic trials to legendary narratives of the discovery of his body, its miraculous journey to Constantinople, and its later translation into France, where his relics heal sufferers from the burning disease associated with his name. The anonymous English versions draw on three distinct Latin hagiographic texts cataloged as BHL 609, 612, and 613.
Part I: life and ascetic formation. Part II: finding and first translation of the relics to Constantinople. Part III: second translation into the Vienne region and cult of Saint Anthony's fire.

assertion.notes: A concise English title distinguishing the three component genres and Antony the Great from Anthony of Padua.

titles.value: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation

notes: Horstmann's heading within the serial article Prosalegenden V.

scope.description: Opening rubric as printed by Horstmann; he argues that it properly belongs to the second component rather than the complete compilation.

titles.value: Here begyns þe fyndyng of þe glorios confessour Antone, þe gret ermyt
Here begynnys þe fyndyng of þe gloryus confessour Antony & grete hermete
Here begynnys þe translacyone of þe gloryus confessor and hermet saynt Antony of Vienensis

work.summary: A three-part Middle English prose compilation about Antony the Great. It moves from an abridged account of his ascetic life and demonic trials to legendary narratives of the discovery of his body, its miraculous journey to Constantinople, and its later translation into France, where his relics heal sufferers from the burning disease associated with his name. The anonymous English versions draw on three distinct Latin hagiographic texts cataloged as BHL 609, 612, and 613.
